summary: O'Neil reflects on a mysterious 'Wanted' poster she found on her street, initially believing it to be a call for vigilante justice against Wall Street wrongdoers. She explores the tension between public frustration over unprosecuted financial crimes and the dangerous history of vigilantism in America. Upon discovering the poster is actually a reproduction of Marcel Duchamp's Dadaist art from the 1920s, she reframes her interpretation. She concludes that while vigilantism is an illusion of justice, the underlying frustration with a broken system is legitimate, and the real solution lies in reforming institutions rather than bypassing them.
key_points: The failure to prosecute Wall Street executives after the 2008 financial crisis has created widespread frustration that echoes conditions historically associated with vigilantism
Vigilantism has both a romanticized cultural presence (Batman, Dexter) and a dark historical reality (lynchings), making it a dangerous response to institutional failure
The poster turned out to be Dadaist art by Marcel Duchamp, transforming O'Neil's reading from literal call-to-action to conceptual commentary
True justice requires fixing broken systems from within, not abandoning them for mob rule
When powerful people consistently escape legal consequences, government and justice risk becoming illusions themselves
money_quotes: Who will really be making decisions about right and wrong if people take law into their own hands – the downtrodden and righteous, or the powerful and corrupt?
Vigilantism is an illusion of justice… but the sense that the system isn't working is still real.
Unless more people within the system actually start to enforce the law against the powerful, then justice and government will lose their authority and become an illusion.
What I (and I assume others) really want is a justice system that works, not one where people have to take the law into their own hands.

A lot of ink has been spilled on how the government has failed to enforce the law with respect to the Financial Crisis and that a collection of the wrong-doers, big and small, have gotten away with it, at the expense of the rest of us.
key_insight: When legal systems consistently fail to hold the powerful accountable, the real danger isn't vigilantism itself but the erosion of faith in justice that makes vigilantism seem appealing.
